title: Rustic X Coffee Table
summary: The coffee-table member of the Rustic X family — 2x6 plank top, 1x12 bottom shelf, and 60°/30° X braces on the ends, all pocket holes and construction lumber.
description:
The Rustic X family (the console and desk are elsewhere in this catalog) gets its living-room anchor here: a low, heavy-looking coffee table with a five-plank 2x6 top, a full 1x12 bottom shelf for the blanket basket, and the signature X braces on each end.

Unlike the desk's scribe-in-place Xs, this plan cuts the X geometry to numbers: the full diagonal at 60° both ends (parallel), and the four half-pieces with one end at 60° and the other at 30°, NOT parallel but cut in the same direction. Read that cut-list line twice and mark the first piece against the saw before committing the batch. The source then lowers the stakes exactly the right amount: the Xs are 'mostly decorative, so don't stress' — good glue and screws, and a hairline gap at a tip is character.

The structural sentence hiding in step 2: this plan is sized for 1x12s measuring a true 11-1/4" — MEASURE YOURS, double it, and cut the side trim to fit, because the bottom shelf is two 1x12s edge-joined and the frame must wrap their actual combined width. Mill variance strikes again; the tape is the truth.

Pocket-hole discipline for visible pieces: top pocket holes hidden on top (under the tabletop), bottom holes facing the floor. And the top builds as its own panel on a flat surface — 1-1/2" holes, 2-1/2" screws — then lands on the base complete.

tools.note: The 60°/30° X cuts. Most saws cut 60° as 30° with the board stood against the fence — test on scrap.
Both settings: 3/4" for the shelf, 1-1/2" for the top.
Handy for tacking the X pieces while glue sets.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Side trim and the X braces.

materials.name: 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: The bottom shelf — measure its true width; the frame is cut to it.

cutList.note: Cut to fit your 1x12s' doubled width — see the description.
Both ends 60° off square, long point to short point, ends PARALLEL.
One end 60°, other end 30°, NOT parallel, cut in the SAME direction. Mark the first against the saw before batching.

steps.title: Build the two end frames
steps.body: Assemble each end: two legs with the 2x4 end trim top and bottom, pocket holes hidden per the doctrine — top joints' holes facing up (the tabletop covers them), bottom joints' holes facing down.

steps.title: Measure the 1x12s, then connect the ends
steps.body: Measure your actual 1x12 width, double it, and cut the 2x2 side trim to wrap that number — the plan assumes a true 11-1/4" and your mill may not. Connect the two end frames with the four side rails, pocket holes hidden as before.

steps.title: Bottom shelf in
steps.body: Edge-join the two 1x12s with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws down the center seam, then drill 3/4" holes around the outside edges and screw the shelf into the frame. The shelf panel is also the base's anti-rack diaphragm — attach all four edges.

steps.title: The X braces
steps.body:
Cut the full diagonal (60°/60°, parallel) and the four half-pieces (60°/30°, not parallel, same direction) and fit an X into each end frame — full diagonal first, halves meeting it at the center.

Then take the source's own pressure release: these are mostly decorative. Good glue, a screw or brad where it lands, and any whisper of gap disappears under stain.

steps.title: Top panel, then together
steps.body:
On a flat, level surface, edge-join the five 2x6 planks with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws, clamping each seam flush. Center the base upside down on the inverted top and screw through the end trim into the planks.

Sand hard — knock the seams flat, ease every edge shins can find — then stain. Books, mugs, and feet arrive immediately; that's what it's for.

images.alt: Rustic coffee table with X-brace ends, plank top, and bottom shelf
